message:
fix(fcp-cbor, fcp-auth-schema): drop redundant clone in validate_decoded_body + readable recursion error

Two small follow-ups to the canonical-depth hardening committed earlier
in this push.

## fcp-cbor

`validate_decoded_body` does a "round-trip canonicalize" sanity pass on
the decoded CBOR to confirm the input already is in canonical form.
The prior implementation was

    let value = decode_cbor_body_as_value(body)?;
    let mut canonical = value.clone();
    canonicalize_value_in_place(&mut canonical, 0)?;

but `value` itself is never read after the clone — the `canonical`
name exists only to satisfy the mutable-borrow requirement of
`canonicalize_value_in_place`. The clone therefore doubles the peak
memory footprint of validation for no observable gain. Rewrite as:

    let mut value = decode_cbor_body_as_value(body)?;
    canonicalize_value_in_place(&mut value, 0)?;

Same semantics: validation still fails when the canonicalization would
reject the tree (over-depth, non-canonical map ordering, non-minimal
int encoding, etc.), because `canonicalize_value_in_place` is the same
function the real canonicalizer calls.

## fcp-auth-schema

`AuthClaims::from_canonical_cbor` previously collapsed every
`ciborium::de::Error` into `SchemaError::Decode(err.to_string())`.
ciborium's `Display` impl for `RecursionLimitExceeded` renders it as
the bare variant name, which in error text reads as
"RecursionLimitExceeded" with no additional context — operators and
downstream clients (who cannot import the ciborium error enum) had no
way to tell that the reject came from the canonical-depth bound we
enforce at parse time.

Replace the blanket closure with an explicit arm that produces a
readable, stable message:

    ciborium::de::Error::RecursionLimitExceeded =>
        SchemaError::Decode(format!(
            "CBOR recursion limit exceeded (max {} levels)",
            fcp_cbor::MAX_DESERIALIZATION_RECURSION_LIMIT,
        ))

All other `ciborium::de::Error` variants continue to use
`err.to_string()` (so "unexpected EOF", "syntax error at offset N",
etc. propagate exactly as before).

Inline comment explains why this branch exists — it is specifically
about making the depth bound discoverable from the error text rather
than buried inside ciborium's variant list. Anyone future-looking at
this code can match on "CBOR recursion limit exceeded" in tests and
logs without reaching back into ciborium internals.

No behavioural change for well-formed inputs.
